label: Apple___Apple_scab
description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It produces olive-green to brown spots on leaves and fruit surfaces leading to reduced fruit quality and yield.
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties. Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn. Ensure good air circulation by pruning trees regularly.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as captan or myclobutanil at bud break and continue through the growing season as directed.

label: Apple___Black_rot
description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes circular brown lesions on leaves and rotting of fruit starting from the blossom end.
prevention: Remove and destroy mummified fruits and dead wood. Prune out infected branches. Avoid wounding trees during maintenance.
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or thiophanate-methyl during the growing season. Remove infected plant material promptly.

label: Apple___Cedar_apple_rust
description: Cedar apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It produces bright orange-yellow spots on apple leaves and can defoliate trees.
prevention: Remove nearby juniper or cedar trees if possible. Plant resistant apple varieties. Apply preventive fungicides in spring.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing myclobutanil or propiconazole starting at pink bud stage and repeat every 7-10 days during wet weather.

label: Apple___healthy
description: The apple plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are green and fruit development is normal.
prevention: Maintain regular pruning for air circulation. Apply balanced fertilizers. Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pests.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine orchard management practices including irrigation and pest monitoring.

label: Blueberry___healthy
description: The blueberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Foliage is vibrant and growth appears normal.
prevention: Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5. Mulch around plants to retain moisture. Prune annually to encourage new growth.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including watering and fertilization with acid-forming fertilizers.

label: Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy
description: The cherry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are glossy green and growth is normal.
prevention: Ensure proper spacing for air circulation. Water at the base to keep foliage dry. Monitor for pests regularly.
treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain good orchard hygiene and continue routine care practices.

label: Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew
description: Powdery mildew on cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina. It appears as white powdery patches on young leaves shoots and fruit causing distortion and reduced quality.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization. Prune to improve air circulation and reduce humidity around the canopy.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides or potassium bicarbonate at first sign of infection. Neem oil can also be effective for mild infections.

label: Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot
description: Gray leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It produces rectangular gray to tan lesions with yellow halos on leaves reducing photosynthesis and yield.
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Rotate crops with non-host plants. Manage crop residue by tillage to reduce inoculum.
treatment: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicides at early tassel stage. Follow label directions and observe pre-harvest intervals.

label: Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_
description: Common rust is caused by Puccinia sorghi. It produces small circular to elongated pustules that are cinnamon-brown on both leaf surfaces reducing photosynthetic area.
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Early planting can help avoid peak rust periods. Monitor fields regularly during the growing season.
treatment: Apply triazole fungicides such as propiconazole at early infection stages. Treatment is most effective before disease spreads widely.

label: Corn_(maize)___healthy
description: The corn plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are dark green and plant development is progressing normally.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Rotate crops annually. Maintain proper plant spacing and balanced soil fertility.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine agronomic practices including fertilization and weed management.

label: Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight
description: Northern leaf blight is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It produces long cigar-shaped grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves that can cause significant yield loss.
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Rotate crops to reduce soil inoculum. Avoid excessive nitrogen and ensure adequate potassium levels.
treatment: Apply triazole or strobilurin fungicides when lesions first appear on lower leaves. Timely application is critical for effectiveness.

label: Grape___Black_rot
description: Grape black rot is caused by Guignardia bidwellii. It causes brown circular lesions on leaves and shrivels fruit into hard black mummies severely reducing harvest.
prevention: Remove and destroy all mummified fruit and infected plant material. Prune vines to improve air circulation. Apply dormant copper sprays.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing mancozeb or myclobutanil starting at bud break. Continue applications every 10-14 days through fruit development.

label: Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles)
description: Esca also known as Black Measles is a complex wood disease involving multiple fungi. It causes tiger-stripe patterns on leaves and dark spots on berries leading to vine decline.
prevention: Avoid large pruning wounds. Seal wounds with wound protectants. Remove and destroy severely infected vines to prevent spread.
treatment: No curative treatment exists. Apply fungicide pastes to pruning wounds as prevention. Manage vine stress through proper irrigation and nutrition.

label: Grape___healthy
description: The grapevine appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are fully developed and fruit clusters are forming normally.
prevention: Train vines properly for good air circulation. Monitor regularly for pests and diseases. Maintain balanced soil nutrition.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine vineyard management including canopy management and balanced fertilization.

label: Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spot)
description: Grape leaf blight caused by Isariopsis clavispora produces dark brown angular lesions on leaves leading to premature defoliation and reduced photosynthesis.
prevention: Remove infected leaves and plant debris. Improve air circulation through canopy management. Avoid overhead irrigation.
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb at the first sign of infection. Repeat applications every 10-14 days during wet seasons.

label: Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening)
description: Huanglongbing or citrus greening is caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter and spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. It causes blotchy yellowing of leaves misshapen bitter fruit and eventual tree death.
prevention: Control Asian citrus psyllid populations with insecticides. Remove and destroy infected trees promptly. Use certified disease-free planting material.
treatment: No cure exists. Manage psyllid vectors aggressively with systemic insecticides. Nutritional sprays can temporarily improve tree appearance but do not cure the disease.

label: Peach___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot on peach is caused by Xanthomonas arboricola. It produces small water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown and fall out giving a shot-hole appearance and affects fruit quality.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid overhead irrigation. Prune to improve air circulation. Avoid working in orchards when wet.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides during dormancy and at early leaf development. Multiple applications may be needed during wet weather.

label: Peach___healthy
description: The peach tree appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are bright green and fruit development is progressing normally.
prevention: Maintain proper pruning for open canopy structure. Monitor for peach leaf curl and other common diseases in spring.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including thinning fruit for better size and quality.

label: Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot on bell pepper is caused by Xanthomonas campestris. It produces small water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit that enlarge and turn brown causing defoliation and yield loss.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Avoid overhead irrigation. Rotate crops away from pepper and tomato. Remove crop debris after harvest.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides preventively. Combined applications with mancozeb can improve effectiveness. Begin at transplanting and continue on a regular schedule.

label: Pepper,_bell___healthy
description: The bell pepper plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Foliage is deep green and fruit set is progressing normally.
prevention: Maintain consistent moisture. Use mulch to prevent soil splash. Monitor regularly for early signs of bacterial or fungal diseases.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including staking plants and balanced fertilization for optimal fruit development.

label: Potato___Early_blight
description: Early blight of potato is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces dark brown to black lesions with concentric rings on older leaves causing premature defoliation and tuber quality issues.
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers. Rotate crops. Maintain adequate soil fertility especially nitrogen. Avoid overhead irrigation.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or mancozeb at first sign of disease. Begin applications when plants reach 6 inches tall as a preventive measure.

label: Potato___healthy
description: The potato plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Foliage is lush green and tuber development is progressing normally.
prevention: Use certified seed potatoes. Hill soil around plants to prevent tuber greening. Monitor for Colorado potato beetle and other pests.
treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain consistent irrigation and fertilization throughout the growing season.

label: Potato___Late_blight
description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans and was responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It causes water-soaked lesions that rapidly turn brown and destroy entire plants in wet conditions.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Use certified disease-free seed. Avoid overhead irrigation. Destroy volunteer potato plants.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil mancozeb or metalaxyl preventively. Begin before disease onset and repeat every 5-7 days during wet weather.

label: Raspberry___healthy
description: The raspberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Canes are vigorous and foliage is a healthy green color.
prevention: Prune out old canes after fruiting. Ensure good drainage and air circulation. Monitor for aphids and spider mites.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including trellising canes and mulching around the base of plants.

label: Soybean___healthy
description: The soybean plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are trifoliate and green with normal nodulation on roots.
prevention: Rotate with non-legume crops. Use certified disease-free seed. Monitor fields regularly for sudden death syndrome and soybean cyst nematode.
treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain optimal planting density and soil fertility for maximum yield potential.

label: Squash___Powdery_mildew
description: Powdery mildew on squash is caused by Podosphaera xanthii. It produces white powdery fungal growth on leaf surfaces causing yellowing premature leaf death and reduced fruit yield.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Space plants for good air circulation. Avoid overhead watering. Remove infected leaves promptly.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides potassium bicarbonate or neem oil at first sign of infection. Repeat every 7-10 days. Baking soda solution can also help.

label: Strawberry___healthy
description: The strawberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are trifoliate and green with normal runner production.
prevention: Renovate beds after harvest. Control weeds. Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry. Mulch with straw to prevent soil splash.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including runner management and fertilization after harvest.

label: Strawberry___Leaf_scorch
description: Leaf scorch on strawberry is caused by Diplocarpon earlianum. It produces small dark purple spots on leaves that enlarge and coalesce causing the leaf to appear scorched and leading to defoliation.
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants. Remove infected leaves. Avoid overhead irrigation. Ensure good air circulation between plants.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as captan or myclobutanil at first sign of disease. Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves and plant debris.

label: Tomato___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot on tomato is caused by Xanthomonas species. It produces small water-soaked spots on leaves stems and fruit that turn brown causing defoliation and fruit blemishes reducing marketability.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants. Avoid overhead irrigation. Rotate crops. Remove and destroy infected plant debris.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides preventively and after rain events. Tank-mix with mancozeb for improved efficacy. Begin at transplanting.

label: Tomato___Early_blight
description: Early blight on tomato is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces dark brown concentric ring lesions on older leaves causing them to yellow and drop reducing plant vigor and yield.
prevention: Rotate crops. Use mulch to prevent soil splash. Remove lower infected leaves. Maintain good plant nutrition especially nitrogen.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil mancozeb or copper fungicides at first sign of disease. Repeat every 7-10 days during wet weather. Remove infected leaves.

label: Tomato___healthy
description: The tomato plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage. Leaves are dark green and fruit development is progressing normally.
prevention: Support plants with stakes or cages. Use drip irrigation. Monitor regularly for early blight late blight and tomato hornworm.
treatment: No treatment needed. Continue routine care including regular fertilization and consistent watering to prevent blossom end rot.

label: Tomato___Late_blight
description: Late blight on tomato is caused by Phytophthora infestans. It rapidly destroys leaves stems and fruit with large brown water-soaked lesions especially in cool wet conditions.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid overhead irrigation. Ensure good air circulation. Remove volunteer tomato and potato plants nearby.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil mancozeb or cymoxanil preventively. Begin before disease onset and reapply every 5-7 days in wet weather.

label: Tomato___Leaf_Mold
description: Leaf mold is caused by Passalora fulva and is most common in greenhouse tomatoes. It produces pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green mold on the underside.
prevention: Reduce humidity in greenhouses. Improve ventilation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove and destroy infected leaves.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or copper-based products at first sign of infection. Improve air circulation immediately.

label: Tomato___Mosaic_virus
description: Tomato mosaic virus causes mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves leaf distortion stunted growth and reduced fruit quality. It is spread by contact and contaminated tools.
prevention: Use resistant varieties. Disinfect tools with bleach solution. Wash hands before handling plants. Control aphid vectors.
treatment: No cure exists for viral infections. Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread. Control insect vectors with appropriate insecticides.

label: Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot
description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by Septoria lycopersici. It produces small circular spots with dark borders and light centers on lower leaves causing progressive defoliation from the bottom up.
prevention: Rotate crops for at least 2 years. Remove infected lower leaves. Use mulch to reduce soil splash. Stake plants for better air circulation.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil mancozeb or copper at first sign of disease. Remove infected leaves before applying fungicide.

label: Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_mite
description: Two-spotted spider mites cause stippled yellowing leaves webbing on plant surfaces and eventual leaf drop. They thrive in hot dry conditions and can rapidly colonize tomato plants.
prevention: Maintain adequate irrigation as drought stress promotes mite outbreaks. Encourage natural predators. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill beneficial insects.
treatment: Apply miticides such as abamectin or bifenazate. Insecticidal soap and neem oil are effective for light infestations. Repeat applications every 5-7 days.

label: Tomato___Target_Spot
description: Target spot on tomato is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It produces brown circular lesions with concentric rings resembling a target on leaves stems and fruit.
prevention: Rotate crops. Remove infected plant debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Maintain good air circulation through proper staking and pruning.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil at first sign of disease. Repeat every 10-14 days. Remove heavily infected leaves.

label: Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus
description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus is transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly. It causes upward leaf curling yellowing stunted growth and severe yield loss. Infected plants rarely recover.
prevention: Use resistant tomato varieties. Control whitefly populations with yellow sticky traps and insecticides. Use reflective mulches to deter whiteflies.
treatment: No cure for the virus. Remove and destroy infected plants. Apply systemic insecticides to control whitefly vectors. Use insect-proof nets in greenhouses.
